elements.sent-2411-001: This paper introduces an analytical formula for the fractional-order conditional moments of nonlinear drift constant elasticity of variance (NLD-CEV) processes under regime switching, governed by continuous-time finite-state irreducible Markov chains.
elements.sent-2411-002: By employing a hybrid system approach, we derive exact closed-form expressions for these moments across arbitrary fractional orders and regime states, thereby enhancing the analytical tractability of NLD-CEV models under stochastic regimes.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our methodology hinges on formulating and solving a complex system of interconnected partial differential equations derived from the Feynman--Kac formula for switching diffusions.
elements.sent-2411-004: To illustrate the practical relevance of our approach, Monte Carlo simulations for process with Markovian switching are applied to validate the accuracy and computational efficiency of the analytical formulas.
elements.sent-2411-005: Furthermore, we apply our findings for the valuation of financial derivatives within a dynamic nonlinear mean-reverting regime-switching framework, which demonstrates significant improvements over traditional methods.
elements.sent-2411-006: This work offers substantial contributions to financial modeling and derivative pricing by providing a robust tool for practitioners and researchers who are dealing with complex stochastic environments.
elements.sent-2411-007: In financial modeling, accurately capturing the stochastic behavior of asset prices is crucial for assessing risk, pricing derivatives, and guiding investment strategies.
elements.sent-2411-008: Standard models, such as the Black--Scholes model, while revolutionary, assume constant volatility and linear dynamics, limiting their ability to address real-world complexities, especially under volatile or turbulent market conditions.
elements.sent-2411-009: To better address these dynamics, researchers have turned to stochastic processes with flexible variance structures and regime-switching mechanisms, which allow models to adapt for shifting economic conditions and unexpected market events.
elements.sent-2411-010: Among these advances, models incorporating constant elasticity of variance (CEV) have gained prominence for their ability to capture changing volatility patterns that depend on the asset price itself.
elements.sent-2411-011: The CEV model introduces a degree of elasticity that makes volatility proportional to the level of the underlying asset, capturing the empirically observed ``leverage effect'' seen in financial markets.
elements.sent-2411-012: However, while the CEV model has brought critical improvements, it remains limited in handling nonlinear dynamics and varying market conditions over time.
elements.sent-2411-013: The nonlinear drift constant elasticity of variance (NLD-CEV) process builds on the flexibility of the CEV model, integrating a nonlinear drift component and regime-switching capability to adapt dynamically across market environments.
elements.sent-2411-014: This process is particularly suited for capturing the asymmetries and conditional heteroscedasticity that are characteristic of asset returns, where market regimes fluctuate according to changing economic conditions.
elements.sent-2411-015: The NLD-CEV model incorporates continuous-time finite-state Markov chains, allowing the process parameters to transition between different states.
elements.sent-2411-016: This regime-switching mechanism enables the model to adjust dynamically, accounting for sudden changes in volatility or drift, which are common in markets influenced by external shocks or cyclical shifts.
elements.sent-2411-017: Our proposed is motivated by the limitations of previous models, such as the extended Cox--Ingersoll--Ross (ECIR) process, which, while incorporating mean-reversion and regime-switching dynamics, relies primarily on linear drift components and lacks the elasticity offered by the NLD-CEV approach.
elements.sent-2411-018: In the ECIR process, the model captures conditional moments through a system of partial differential equations (PDEs) under Markov switching, enabling applications in VIX options pricing.
elements.sent-2411-019: However, ECIR\rq s linearity may limit its application in scenarios requiring greater adaptability to changing market volatility.
elements.sent-2411-020: The NLD-CEV model addresses this gap by introducing a nonlinear drift term, which allows for more robust modeling of asymmetrical market responses and variance elasticity.
elements.sent-2411-021: In this paper, we develop an analytical framework to calculate fractional-order conditional moments for the NLD-CEV process with regime switching.
elements.sent-2411-022: Using a hybrid system approach, we derive closed-form solutions for these moments, enhancing both the analytical tractability and computational efficiency of the model.
elements.sent-2411-023: Our approach is grounded in the Feynman--Kac formula, adapted for regime-switching diffusion processes, leading to a recursive system of PDEs for the fractional moments.
elements.sent-2411-024: We also explore the model’s asymptotic properties in a two-state regime-switching scenario, assessing how variations in the Markov chain intensity matrix and parameter configurations affect the conditional moments and overall process behavior.
elements.sent-2411-025: This analysis demonstrates the ability of the NLD-CEV model to handle a wide range of market scenarios, extending its applicability beyond that of traditional models.
elements.sent-2411-026: To validate the efficacy of our approach, we conduct Monte Carlo simulations, comparing the performance of our closed-form solutions to traditional computational methods.
elements.sent-2411-027: The results illustrate not only the accuracy but also the computational efficiency of our analytical formulas in capturing conditional moments.
elements.sent-2411-028: Moreover, we apply the NLD-CEV model for the valuation of financial derivatives, such as options, highlighting its practical relevance and superiority over traditional models in capturing market complexities.
elements.sent-2411-029: Our findings present a valuable contribution to the fields of financial modeling and derivative pricing, equipping researchers and practitioners with a powerful tool for navigating stochastic environments characterized by nonlinear dynamics and regime-switching behavior.
elements.sent-2411-030: The paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-031: In Section~\ref{sec_Regime , we define the nonlinear drift constant elasticity of variance (NLD-CEV) process with regime switching, setting up the necessary framework and assumptions that underpin our model.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Section~\ref{sec_CM delves into the hybrid system of partial differential equations (PDEs) derived from the Feynman--Kac formula for switching diffusions, laying out the analytical foundation for computing fractional-order conditional moments under a regime-switching structure.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Moreover, we present in this section the main analytical results, including explicit closed-form formulas for conditional moments, and examine the influence of different parameter configurations and Markov chain states.
elements.sent-2411-034: Section~\ref{sec_exper provides a method for stochastic differential equations with Markovian switching and validates our theoretical findings through Monte Carlo simulations, comparing the efficiency and accuracy of the formulas against conventional numerical methods.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Finally, Section~\ref{sec_application discusses practical applications of the NLD-CEV model in financial contexts, particularly in pricing derivatives such as VIX options, illustrating the model\rq s adaptability to real-world financial data.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Section~\ref{sec_conclusion concludes the paper with a summary of our findings and potential directions for future research.}
elements.sent-2411-037: The constant elasticity of variance (CEV) diffusion process, first introduced by Cox \cite{cox1975notes in 1975, is viewed as an extension of the Ornstein--Uhlenbeck (OU) process, particularly for applications in finance.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Since its inception, the CEV model has been explored and expanded across various fields.
elements.sent-2411-039: In recent studies, Araneda et al. \cite{araneda2021sub investigated the sub-fractional CEV model, while while Cao et al. \cite{cao2021pricing examined variance swap pricing under a hybrid CEV-stochastic volatility framework, demonstrating the model's adaptability to contemporary financial challenges.}}
elements.subsection-2411-001: Nonlinear drift constant elasticity of variance process
elements.sent-2411-040: The first generalized version of Cox’s CEV process was proposed by Marsh and Rosenfeld \cite{marsh1983stochastic , who incorporated time-dependent parameters and a nonlinear drift term, resulting in what we now call the nonlinear drift CEV (NLD-CEV) process as in~\cite{sutthimat20222closed .}}
elements.sent-2411-041: The NLD-CEV model can be formulated as: \begin{equation}\label{main_process} dR_t = \kappa(t) \Big(\theta(t)R_t^{-(1-\beta)} - R_t \Big) dt + \sigma(t)R_t^{\beta/2} dW_t, \quad \beta \in [0,2) \cup (2, \infty),\end{equation} where $\theta(t)$, $\kappa(t)$, and $\sigma(t)$ are time-dependent parameters over $t \in [t_0, T]$ with initial value $R_{t_0 > 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Here, the diffusion term $\sigma(t)R_t^{\beta/2}$ aligns with the structure in Cox\rq s original CEV model, but the drift term $\kappa(t)\big(\theta(t)R_t^{-(1-\beta) - R_t\big)$ introduces nonlinearity, distinguishing it from the traditional formulation.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Varying the parameter $\beta$ produces several well-known processes within the NLD-CEV framework.
elements.sent-2411-044: When $\beta = 1$, the model becomes an extended Cox–Ingersoll–Ross (ECIR) process, demonstrating mean-reversion characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-045: For $\beta = 0$, it aligns with the OU process, while for $\beta \rightarrow 2$, it approximates the lognormal process studied by Merton.
elements.sent-2411-046: Furthermore, for $\beta \in (0, \infty)$, the NLD-CEV process exhibits mean-reversion, and when $\beta = 3$, it corresponds to the inverse Feller (or 3/2) stochastic volatility model or 3/2-SVM.
elements.sent-2411-047: Evidence suggests that nonlinearity in the drift component of the NLD-CEV model is particularly well-suited for modeling the behavior of financial derivatives, especially those influenced by interest rate dynamics~\cite{zhou2003ito .}
elements.sent-2411-048: For instance, empirical findings by Chan et al. demonstrate that models with $\beta > 2$ more effectively capture short-term rate movements compared to models where $\beta < 2$.
elements.sent-2411-049: The NLD-CEV process is divided into two cases, each characterized by a distinct range of values for $\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-050: In the first case, where $0 \leq \beta < 2$, we set $\beta = \frac{2\alpha - 1 {\alpha}$, and the process becomes \begin{equation}\label{main_process<2} dR_t = \kappa(t) \Big( \theta(t)R_t^{\frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha}} - R_t \Big) dt + \sigma(t)R_t^{\frac{2\alpha - 1}{2\alpha}} dW_t, \quad \alpha \geq \frac{1}{2}.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-114: The conditional $\frac{n {\alpha}$-moment for $n\in\mathbb{Z ^+_0$ is \begin{equation}\label{cm1} U_{\alpha,i}^{\langle n\rangle}(\tau,R) = \mathbf{E}^{\mathbb{Q}}\Big[ R_T^{\frac{n}{\alpha}} ~|~ R_t= R, X_t = i \Big] = \mathds{1}_{ \{1\} }(i)~ \sum_{k=0}^n A_\alpha^{\langle k\rangle}(\tau)R^\frac{k}{\alpha} + \mathds{1}_{ \{2\} }(i)~ \sum_{k=0}^n B_\alpha^{\langle k\rangle}(\tau)R^\frac{k}{\alpha},\end{equation} where $\tau = T-t\geq0$, $\mathds{1}$ is the indicator function, $A_\alpha^{\langle k\rangle}$ and $B_\alpha^{\langle k\rangle}$ for $k=0,1,\ldots,n$ can be solved by the system of recursive matrix differential equation: \begin{equation}\mathbf{\overline{u}}_\alpha^{\langle n\rangle}(\tau) = P_\alpha^{\langle n\rangle} u_\alpha^{\langle n\rangle}(\tau) \label{311}, \mathbf{\overline{u}}_\alpha^{\langle \ell\rangle}(\tau) = P_\alpha^{\langle \ell\rangle} u_\alpha^{\langle \ell\rangle}(\tau) + D_\alpha^{\langle \ell\rangle} u_\alpha^{\langle \ell+1\rangle}(\tau), \label{312}\end{equation} with the initial conditions $\mathbf{u _\alpha^{\langle n\rangle (0)=[1,1]^\top$ and $\mathbf{u _\alpha^{\langle \ell\rangle (0)=[0,0]^\top~\mbox{for ~\ell=0,1,\ldots,n-1$, where \begin{equation}\mathbf{\overline{u}}_\alpha^{\langle k\rangle}(\tau) =  \frac{d}{d\tau}A_\alpha^{\langle k\rangle}(\tau) \frac{d}{d\tau}B_\alpha^{\langle k\rangle}(\tau) , \quad P_\alpha^{\langle k\rangle} =  -\kappa_1\frac{k}{\alpha}-q_{12} q_{12} q_{21} -\kappa_2\frac{k}{\alpha}-q_{21} , \quad u_\alpha^{\langle k\rangle}(\tau) =  A_\alpha^{\langle k\rangle}(\tau) B_\alpha^{\langle k\rangle}(\tau) , D_\alpha^{\langle \ell\rangle} = diag\left\{\gamma_{\alpha,1}^{\langle\ell\rangle},\gamma_{\alpha,2}^{\langle\ell\rangle}\right\}, \qquad \mbox{and} \quad \gamma_{\alpha,i}^{\langle\ell\rangle} = \left(\frac{\ell+1}{\alpha}\right)\left(\kappa_i\theta_i+\frac{1}{2}\sigma_i^2\left(\frac{\ell+1}{\alpha}-1\right)\right).\end{equation}. \end{Theorem.}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-160: A pertinent question for market practitioners concerns the accuracy and computational efficiency of these newly derived formulas.
elements.sent-2411-161: To address this, results generated from the formulas are compared against those obtained through Monte Carlo simulations.
elements.sent-2411-162: For the hybrid system implementation, the EM method tailored for SDEs with Markovian switching, as developed by Yuan and Mao~\cite{yuan2004convergence , is applied to simulate approximate solutions for SDEs with switching regimes.}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \setstretch{1.15} \caption{Simulation of continuous-time Markov chain and EM approximation for $2$ states} \label{algSimu} \floatname{algorithm}{Procedure} \renewcommand{\algorithmicrequire}{Input:} \renewcommand{\algorithmicensure}{Output:} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Generator matrix $Q$, time step $h$, terminal time $T$, initial state $i_0$, initial value $R_{t_0}$, parameters $\kappa$, $\theta_{X_k}$, $\sigma_{X_k}$, $\beta$ \Ensure Simulated discrete Markov chain $\{ X_k, \, k = 0, 1, \dots, k^* \}$ and $\widehat{R}_k$ \State Calculate the one-step transition probability matrix $P(h) = e^{hQ}$ \State Set \ $t_0 = 0$ \ and \ $k = 0$ \State Set \ $X_0 = i_0$ \ and \ $\widehat{R}_0 = R_{t_0}$ \While {\ $t_k \leq T$} \State Generate a uniformly distributed random number $\omega \in [0, 1]$ \If { \ $\omega \leq P_{i_k 1}$} \State $X_{k+1} = 1$ \Else \State $X_{k+1} = 2$ \EndIf \State Compute \ $\widehat{R}_{k+1} = \widehat{R}_k + \kappa \left(\theta_{X_k} \widehat{R}_k^{-(1-\beta)} - \widehat{R}_k \right)h + \sigma_{X_k} \widehat{R}_k^{\frac{\beta}{2}} \sqrt{h} \, Z_k$ \State Update \ $i_{k + 1} = X_{k+1}$ \State Update \ $t_{k+1} = t_k + h$ \State Update \ $k = k + 1$ \EndWhile \State \Return \ Simulated discrete Markov chain $\{ X_k, \, k = 0, 1, \dots, k^* \}$ and $\widehat{R}_k$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.section-2411-005: Financial applications on VIX option pricing
elements.sent-2411-184: The VIX, a 30-day implied volatility measure derived from S\&P 500 index options, is essential for assessing market sentiment and managing volatility risk.
elements.sent-2411-185: Accurate pricing of VIX derivatives depends on using a model that captures the complex nature of volatility.
elements.sent-2411-186: This study presents a novel approach to VIX option pricing, introducing a closed-form formula based on the NLD-CEV model with regime switching.
elements.sent-2411-187: The NLD-CEV model offers several advantages over traditional models, such as the Cox–Ingersoll–Ross (CIR) model, by incorporating mean reversion and enabling data-driven estimation of the elasticity parameter \(\beta\).
elements.sent-2411-188: This flexibility addresses a key limitation of commonly used models like the square root and 3/2 models, which assume fixed values of \(\beta\) at 1 and 3, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-189: Empirical evidence suggests that \(\beta\) often falls outside this range, underscoring the need for a model that allows for data-driven parameter estimation \mbox{\cite{dupoyet2011simplified, tong2017modelling .}}
elements.sent-2411-190: Leveraging the theoretical basis of the NLD-CEV model, this research develops a VIX option pricing formula with practical implications.
elements.sent-2411-191: The ability to derive a closed-form solution for fractional-order conditional moments in the NLD-CEV process enables efficient and accurate option price calculations.
elements.sent-2411-192: This methodology bridges the gap between advanced mathematical modeling and practical financial applications, significantly enhancing option pricing techniques for volatility derivatives.
elements.sent-2411-193: This section applies the derived closed-form formula to price VIX options within a 2-state regime-switching NLD-CEV framework.
elements.sent-2411-194: The focus is on European call options on the VIX index, a critical tool in volatility trading and risk management.
elements.sent-2411-195: Consider a European call option with maturity date \( T \) and strike price \( K \).
elements.sent-2411-196: The fair value of this option at any time \( t \in [0, T] \) is defined by \begin{equation}C_T(t, R, K, i) = e^{-r^*\tau} \mathbf{E}^\mathbb{Q}[\max(R_T - K, 0) \mid R_t = R, X_t = i] \label{eq:vix_option_price}\end{equation} where \( r^* \) is the risk-free rate, \( \tau = T - t \) denotes the time to maturity, \( R_t \) represents the VIX level at time \( t \), and \( X_t \) indicates the regime state at time \( t \).

elements.sent-2411-197: To efficiently compute the expectation in Equation \eqref{eq:vix_option_price , the Laguerre expansion method by Dufresne~\cite{Dufrense2000option is employed.}}
elements.sent-2411-198: This approach expresses the European call option price as an infinite series, \begin{equation}C_T(t, R, K, i) = e^{-r^*\tau} K^b e^{-K} \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} c_j(t, R, T, i) \, \mathbf{L}_j^a(K), \label{eq:laguerre_expansion}\end{equation} where \( a, b \in \mathbb{R \) satisfy \( a > 2\max(b, 0) - 1 \), and \( \mathbf{L _j^a(\cdot) \) denotes the generalized Laguerre polynomial of order \( j \) with parameter \( a > -1 \).}}

elements.sent-2411-199: The coefficients \( c_j \) are given in terms of the conditional moments of \( R_T \), \begin{equation}c_j(t, R, T, i) = \sum_{n=0}^j \frac{j! (-1)^n \ \mathbf{E}^\mathbb{Q}\Big[R_T^{\frac{\gamma_n}{\alpha}} \mid R_t = r, X_t = i\Big]}{\Gamma(n + a + 1) n! (j-n)! (\frac{\gamma_n}{\alpha} - 1) \frac{\gamma_n}{\alpha}} \label{eq:laguerre_coefficients}\end{equation} for \( j \in \mathbb{N _0 \), where \( \frac{\gamma_n {\alpha = a - b + n + 2 \).}}}

elements.sent-2411-200: Implementing this option pricing formula involves several steps.
elements.sent-2411-201: First, suitable values for \( a \) and \( b \) are chosen to satisfy \( a > 2\max(b, 0) - 1 \) and ensure that \( \frac{\gamma_n {\alpha \in \mathbb{N \) for all \( n \in [0, j] \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-202: This choice of parameters guarantees the convergence of the Laguerre series and facilitates calculation of the coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-203: Next, the closed-form formula for the NLD-CEV process expectation is used to obtain the moments \( \mathbf{E ^\mathbb{Q}\Big[R_T^{\frac{\gamma_n {\alpha \mid R_t = R, X_t = i\Big] \).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-204: These moments are subsequently applied to calculate the coefficients \( c_j \) in~\eqref{eq:laguerre_coefficients .}
elements.sent-2411-205: Finally, the option price is computed using a truncated version of the series in~\eqref{eq:laguerre_expansion .}
elements.sent-2411-206: This method provides substantial benefits in VIX option pricing, including fast computation and high accuracy enabled by closed-form solutions.
elements.sent-2411-207: By aligning with the NLD-CEV framework, this approach ensures that the option pricing model accurately reflects the VIX dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-208: Applying these theoretical findings to a practical quantitative finance problem demonstrates potential improvements in pricing and hedging strategies for volatility trading and risk management.
elements.sent-2411-209: This work advances the development of sophisticated option pricing methodologies, linking rigorous mathematical modeling with tangible financial applications.
elements.sent-2411-210: In this study, we presented an analytical framework for the fractional-order conditional moments of the NLD-CEV process with regime-switching dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-211: By integrating a nonlinear drift component and accommodating elasticity in variance, our approach offers a versatile modeling tool for financial markets, especially in pricing derivatives like VIX options, where volatility and market shifts play critical roles.
elements.sent-2411-212: The hybrid system approach adopted here allows for exact closed-form solutions, which significantly enhances computational efficiency compared to traditional Monte Carlo simulations.
elements.sent-2411-213: Our numerical results underscore the computational advantages of the closed-form formulas derived in this study.
elements.sent-2411-214: In particular, we observe substantial reductions in computational time, with accuracy that aligns closely with MC simulation outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-215: This computational efficiency is invaluable in practical applications where rapid pricing and risk assessment are essential.
elements.sent-2411-216: Furthermore, our results demonstrate that the NLD-CEV model captures nuanced dynamics by adjusting to changes in economic conditions through regime-switching, thereby providing more accurate reflections of market realities than many traditional models.
elements.sent-2411-217: The applications explored, specifically in VIX options and options pricing, highlight the practical utility of our model in financial engineering.
elements.sent-2411-218: The model\rq s adaptability to shifts in market sentiment and volatility makes it well-suited for environments characterized by sudden economic changes.
elements.sent-2411-219: By incorporating regime-switching elements, the NLD-CEV model not only enhances accuracy in volatility modeling but also addresses some limitations inherent in simpler processes by capturing nonlinear responses to market shocks.
elements.sent-2411-220: Future research could extend this work by exploring multi-regime settings and incorporating additional stochastic elements to further refine the model\rq s adaptability to market complexities.
elements.sent-2411-221: Additionally, empirical validation using a broader dataset of financial instruments could provide insights into optimizing model parameters for specific financial contexts.
elements.sent-2411-222: This research contributes a significant step toward more efficient and accurate pricing methodologies, bridging advanced stochastic modeling techniques with concrete financial applications.
